metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add automated grep assertions to AC3 (or to `gen_mcp_reference.py --check`) that verify `sign_review` appears under the Write-gated section (not LLM-gated), `reconcile` is annotated with `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC`, and `fsck` is annotated with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` — e.g. `grep -A5 'Write-gated' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'sign_review' || echo MISSING`.
findings.finding: The three special-case classification rules — `sign_review` in write tier not LLM tier, `reconcile` annotated jira-gated, `fsck` annotated readonly-gated — are verified only by 'reading the generated sections' (a manual step), not by any automated assertion. A bug in the special-case table would pass CI undetected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'Verify by reading the generated sections.'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add three one-liner shell assertions to AC3 (and mirror them in the CI job):
```
grep -A50 'Write-gated' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'sign_review' || echo 'FAIL: sign_review misclassified'
grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' docs/mcp-reference.md && grep -B2 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'reconcile' || echo 'FAIL: reconcile annotation missing'
grep -B2 -A2 'fsck' docs/mcp-reference.md | grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_READONLY' || echo 'FAIL: fsck annotation missing'
```

findings.finding: AC2 ('The reference correctly separates read-only from write/live-gated tools') has no independent verification mechanism. The only CI check — the drift gate — compares fresh generation against committed output, but both are produced by the same generator logic. If `gen_mcp_reference.py` misclassifies a tool (e.g., puts a write tool in the read-only section due to a wrong flag check), the drift gate stays green forever because it never compares against an independent oracle of which tools belong to which profile. This is the self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: the test locks in the generator's own (possibly wrong) classification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 / scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` or a companion test, add a hardcoded fixture table (e.g. `EXPECTED_PROFILES = {'some_write_tool': 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'some_readonly_tool': 'readonly'}`) and assert the generated markdown places each fixture tool in the correct section. Wire this assertion into CI as a separate step from the drift gate.

findings.finding: AC4 says adding/removing an MCP tool 'changes the generated output (verified)' but the plan body describes no mechanism — test, script flag, or CI step — that specifically exercises add/remove behavior to verify the output changes; the drift gate alone only checks committed vs regenerated, not that the generator is sensitive to tool-list mutations.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'Adding/removing an MCP tool changes the generated output (verified).'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan body, add: 'A unit test in `tests/test_gen_mcp_reference.py` calls the generator with a synthetic tool list of N tools, then again with N+1, and asserts the outputs differ — satisfying AC4.' Then update AC4 to reference that test.

findings.finding: AC4 states 'Adding/removing an MCP tool changes the generated output (verified)' but names no concrete verification mechanism — no test, no explicit CI step, no manual procedure is specified. 'Verified' is asserted without a proof path, leaving the criterion unobservable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pytest test in scripts/tests/test_gen_mcp_reference.py that monkeypatches list_tools() to add/remove a dummy tool entry, runs gen_mcp_reference.py, and asserts the emitted docs/mcp-reference.md contains/omits that entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either pin the exact Markdown table-row format the generator must emit (e.g., `| `tool_name` | gate | description |`) and update the count-proof regex to match it exactly, or replace the grep-based count with a more robust approach (e.g., a Python assertion in the generator's --check mode that counts registered tool names directly).
findings.finding: The plan's AC count-proof command uses the regex `grep -cE '^\| ?`?[a-z_]+`? '` to count tool rows, but the plan never pins the Markdown table-row format that gen_mcp_reference.py will emit. If the generator uses a different column format (e.g., bold names, different spacing, or no backticks), the regex silently fails to count rows and the AC becomes a non-deterministic false negative rather than a reliable gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 4 (Count proof)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies `.github/workflows/test.yml` contains a step invoking `gen_mcp_reference.py --check` (e.g. `grep -q 'gen_mcp_reference' .github/workflows/test.yml || echo MISSING`).
findings.finding: The `.github/workflows/test.yml` CI gate is a described deliverable (Scope + Files list) but has no acceptance criterion that verifies it was actually added — an implementer could skip the CI step and pass all ACs.
findings.location: Scope: '--check drift mode + CI gate in .github/workflows/test.yml'; Files list: '.github/workflows/test.yml'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `grep -q 'gen_mcp_reference.py --check' .github/workflows/test.yml || echo 'CI GATE MISSING'`

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fourth string to the AC section-verify grep command (e.g. 'Jira-gated' or 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC') so all four Scope-declared sections are gated, resolving the Scope↔AC contradiction.
findings.finding: The Scope section specifies FOUR clearly-labeled sections in the generated doc (Read-only, LLM-gated, Write-gated, and jira-gated reconcile), but the AC section-structure verify command only checks for THREE section headings ('Read-only', 'LLM-gated', 'Write-gated'), leaving the fourth section unverified and creating a contradiction between what the generator must produce and what is gated.
findings.location: Scope (FOUR sections) vs AC bullet 2 (grep command checking three strings)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC verify loop: `for s in 'Read-only' 'LLM-gated' 'Write-gated' 'Jira-gated'; do grep -qi "$s" docs/mcp-reference.md || echo "NO SECTION $s"; done`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism gen_mcp_reference.py uses to apply the classification override table (e.g., 'use a hardcoded OVERRIDES dict keyed by tool name, mapping each of the three special-case tools to their target section and annotation') so the processing protocol is unambiguous and deterministic.
findings.finding: The classification rule says 'classify by which registrar registers the tool, then apply the closed override table,' but never specifies the lookup MECHANISM the generator must use to apply the three special-case overrides — specifically whether gen_mcp_reference.py should use hardcoded tool-name string matching, source introspection, or some other method. Without this, an implementor producing gen_mcp_reference.py cannot deterministically implement the override step, making the processing protocol incomplete.
findings.location: Plan section 'Classification rule' / 'Scope: Add scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Apply overrides via a hardcoded `TOOL_OVERRIDES` dict in the generator: `{"sign_review": ("Write-gated", "REBAR_MCP_READONLY"), "reconcile": ("Read-only", "REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC annotated"), "fsck": ("Read-only", "REBAR_MCP_READONLY when recover=True")}`. After enumerating each registrar, re-classify any tool whose name appears in TOOL_OVERRIDES before emitting sections.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying that the generated doc contains at least one MCP_ENV_VARS-sourced gate description string (e.g. grep for 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM' or 'REBAR_MCP_READONLY' in the output doc body, not just headers).
findings.finding: The Scope requires including `MCP_ENV_VARS` gate descriptions in the generated doc, but no AC verifies their presence — this described deliverable is orphaned.
findings.location: Scope: 'Include the MCP_ENV_VARS gate descriptions'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM' docs/mcp-reference.md && grep -q 'REBAR_MCP_READONLY' docs/mcp-reference.md || echo 'GATE DESCRIPTIONS MISSING'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a comment block in `gen_mcp_reference.py` adjacent to the special-case table that explicitly lists the override entries and instructs maintainers to update it when adding cross-gate tools; optionally assert the table covers a known set of tool names so an unrecognized override name fails loudly.
findings.finding: The special-case classification table (reconcile → JIRA-gated, fsck → readonly, sign_review → write-gated) is hardcoded in the generator with no mechanism to detect when a newly added cross-gate tool is missing from the table — the drift gate catches count/content changes but not misclassifications of existing tool counts.
findings.location: Scope section — classification rule table; scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py (new)
findings.suggested_fix: In gen_mcp_reference.py, define the override table as a named constant with an inline comment: `# CLOSED TABLE — update when adding any tool whose gate differs from its registrar default`. Optionally add an assertion: `assert all(name in all_tool_names for name in SPECIAL_CASES)` so a renamed tool causes a visible failure rather than a silent miss.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `--check` behavior when `docs/mcp-reference.md` is absent: either treat absence as drift (exit non-zero with a clear message) or auto-generate then re-check, and add an AC covering this case.
findings.finding: The `--check` mode behavior when `docs/mcp-reference.md` does not yet exist is unspecified. The script could crash with an unhandled FileNotFoundError, silently pass (a false MISS), or emit a confusing traceback — none of which is specified or tested by the AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — drift gate AC / `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (new)
findings.suggested_fix: In `gen_mcp_reference.py --check`: if the target file does not exist, print `docs/mcp-reference.md not found — run gen_mcp_reference.py to generate it` and exit 1. Add a one-line AC: 'Running `--check` when `docs/mcp-reference.md` is absent exits non-zero with a human-readable message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` to the Files edit-set and include the `gen_mcp_reference.py --check` drift gate step there, mirroring the test.yml addition (required by `test_ci_workflow_parity.py::test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci`).
findings.finding: .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml is an unenumerated consumer that requires updating. The plan's Files section lists only .github/workflows/test.yml, but tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py::test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci scans test.yml for all 'scripts/*.py' references and asserts each also appears in gerrit-verify.yaml (with an empty _INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES set). Adding the gen_mcp_reference.py drift gate to test.yml without mirroring it in gerrit-verify.yaml will fail this parity test and block CI.
findings.location: Files section: `.github/workflows/test.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` to the Files section. The new step in gerrit-verify.yaml should mirror the test.yml step: `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py && git diff --exit-code -- docs/mcp-reference.md`. Also verify that `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` in test_ci_workflow_parity.py gets an entry for this gate (or confirm the script-regex test covers it automatically).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` to the plan's file scope: wire the `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` drift gate into `gerrit-verify.yaml` (mirroring every other drift-gate script), and add a `'mcp-reference drift gate': 'scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py'` entry to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` in `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`.
findings.finding: Adding `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to `test.yml` without also updating `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` will immediately fail the `test_no_drift_script_gate_is_verified_only_in_branch_ci` test, breaking the 'make test stays green' AC. The parity-enforcement test uses a regex scan of `test.yml` for `scripts/*.py` references and asserts each appears verbatim in `gerrit-verify.yaml`; `_INTENTIONAL_SCRIPT_ASYMMETRIES` is currently empty and provides no exemption. Additionally, `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES` in that test file would need a new entry for the MCP reference drift gate.
findings.location: Files section / AC line 'make lint && make typecheck && make test stay green' / tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Files: `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (add MCP-reference drift gate step mirroring the env-registry gate), `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` (add `'mcp-reference drift gate': 'scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py'` to `_SHARED_GATE_SIGNATURES`). The gate step in gerrit-verify.yaml can be identical in structure to the existing env-var registry gate step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief entry in CLAUDE.md (or the project README) pointing to `docs/mcp-reference.md` and noting that `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py --check` is the drift gate, mirroring the env-registry pattern.
findings.finding: The plan creates docs/mcp-reference.md and scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py but does not mention updating CLAUDE.md, README, or any index to make the new reference discoverable or to document the generator-pattern convention for future contributors.
findings.location: Scope section (Files list); docs/mcp-reference.md (new)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `gen_mcp_reference.py` writes to a temp file in the same directory and atomically renames it to `docs/mcp-reference.md` to prevent partial-write corruption.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify atomic file writing for `docs/mcp-reference.md`. A plain `open(..., 'w')` write that is interrupted mid-way (e.g., disk full, KeyboardInterrupt) leaves a truncated/corrupt file committed in the repo, causing all subsequent `--check` runs to report false drift or error.
findings.location: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` (new) — file I/O section
findings.suggested_fix: Write output to `docs/mcp-reference.md.tmp`, then `os.replace('docs/mcp-reference.md.tmp', 'docs/mcp-reference.md')` — `os.replace` is atomic on POSIX and best-effort on Windows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (or replace) the per-registrar FastMCP instantiation approach: confirm that calling `register_read_tools(bare_FastMCP, ctx)` / `register_write_tools` / `register_llm_tools` with a manually-constructed `ctx` namespace works correctly — OR switch to the already-proven `asyncio.run(build_server().list_tools())` path (with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0` injected into the environment before the call) and apply the classification table post-hoc.
findings.finding: The plan's proposed enumeration mechanism — 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0' — is a novel per-registrar instantiation approach not demonstrated anywhere in the codebase. Every existing tool enumeration in the repo uses asyncio.run(build_server().list_tools()), which goes through the full ctx assembly (including _load_engine_mode() for MODE_CAPS/Mode, the _readonly/_allow_llm/_allow_jira_sync callables, etc.). Calling register_read_tools/register_llm_tools/register_write_tools directly on a bare FastMCP without a correctly-populated ctx namespace will crash. The plan does not verify that the per-registrar approach is viable, nor justify why it is preferred over the already-proven build_server() + list_tools() path.
findings.location: Scope section: 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0'
findings.suggested_fix: In gen_mcp_reference.py, use the established pattern: set os.environ['REBAR_MCP_READONLY'] = '0', then call asyncio.run(build_server().list_tools()) to obtain all 47 tools, then classify each tool name against the closed override table (registrar membership can be determined by checking the tool name against the known sets from each registrar module, or by temporarily monkey-patching each registrar to record its registrations). This avoids duplicating the ctx assembly logic and stays in sync with build_server() automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism for forcing write-tool registration during generation (e.g., 'set os.environ["REBAR_MCP_READONLY"] = "0" and os.environ["REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM"] = "1" at generator startup, before any import that calls build_server()') so the generator is environment-independent and deterministic.
findings.finding: The plan says the generator must enumerate registrars 'with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 (so writes register),' but register_write_tools() reads ctx.readonly() which calls rebar.config.mcp_readonly() — going through the real config stack including env vars and config files. The plan never specifies the MECHANISM for forcing REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 during generation (patch os.environ before calling build_server()? build a synthetic ctx with a lambda: False readonly? mock config?). This is a critical sub-step with an unspecified implementation path.
findings.location: Plan section 'Scope: Add scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py' / 'enumerate each registrar onto its own FastMCP with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'At the top of gen_mcp_reference.py, before calling build_server(), force os.environ["REBAR_MCP_READONLY"] = "0" to ensure write tools register regardless of ambient environment. Also document that the generator must install the mcp extra (or skip registration of tools that require it) or use per-registrar direct call with a mock FastMCP.'

findings.finding: The plan's 'read-only vs write (live) profiles' binary framing is inconsistent with the actual three-tier gating in the codebase, most critically because sign_review is in the LLM registrar (_mcp_llm.py) but is gated ONLY by REBAR_MCP_READONLY (not REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM). A generator that naively partitions by registrar file will misclassify sign_review as LLM-gated when it is actually write-gated.
findings.location: AC2: 'The reference correctly separates read-only from write/live-gated tools'; What section: 'MUST distinguish the read-only vs write (live) profiles'

findings.finding: The plan's proposed mechanism of enumerating tools from `list_tools()` at runtime is broken for producing a complete, gate-annotated reference: write-tools are conditionally REGISTERED (not just gated at call-time) — `register_write_tools` returns early when `_readonly()` is true at `build_server()` time, so a single server invocation produces an incomplete list. To enumerate all tools across all profiles the generator would need to call `build_server()` multiple times with different env-flag combinations, a combinatorially fragile approach the plan does not specify. The existing `gen_env_registry.py` pattern the plan says it templates uses static AST scanning, which sidesteps this problem entirely.
findings.location: What section — 'enumerate tools from the MCP server's `list_tools()` registry'
findings.suggested_fix: In `gen_mcp_reference.py`, force all gate lambdas to return False for `_readonly` (expose all write tools) and True for `_allow_llm`/`_allow_jira_sync` (expose all LLM/sync tools) when calling `build_server()`, or enumerate tools by directly calling each `register_read_tools`/`register_llm_tools`/`register_write_tools` against a stub FastMCP with a controlled ctx. Then annotate each tool with which env flag gates it based on which registrar registered it.

findings.finding: The plan proposes enumerating tools via `list_tools()` at generator runtime, but `register_write_tools` returns early (no-op) when `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1`, and `register_llm_tools` tools are always registered but their gate metadata is only in docstrings/source — not in `list_tools()` output. Running the generator in a read-only environment silently produces an incomplete tool list (write tools absent); running it without knowing each tool's gate env var cannot reconstruct which tools are gated by which flag. The codebase already has two relevant static sources: `MCP_ENV_VARS` (env-var contract) and `tool_annotation_presets()` (readOnlyHint/destructiveHint per category), plus per-tool gate metadata only in docstrings. The plan commits to a runtime-enumeration architecture without acknowledging this conditional-registration problem or the static alternatives, and does not specify the env-var configuration under which `build_server()` will be called.
findings.location: Plan body: 'enumerate tools from the MCP server's `list_tools()` registry'; `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` lines 33-40; `src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py` lines 8-9

findings.finding: The plan says the generator enumerates tools from `src/rebar/_mcp` (or equivalent), but this path does not exist. The actual server is built via `build_server()` in `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` assembling three registrars (_mcp_reads, _mcp_llm, _mcp_writes). More critically, write tools are conditionally REGISTERED: `_mcp_writes.py` line 38 states they 'never appear in list_tools()' when REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1. The plan never specifies that the generator must call `build_server()` with readonly mode disabled (i.e., REBAR_MCP_READONLY unset) to get a complete tool list — an executor following the plan as-written may invoke `list_tools()` in whatever ambient env state CI uses, silently producing a partial reference that passes the drift gate.
findings.location: Plan body: 'enumerate tools from the MCP server's `list_tools()` registry (`src/rebar/_mcp` or equivalent)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The generator MUST call `build_server()` (from `rebar.mcp_server`) with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` unset (or set to 0) so all three tool clusters are registered and appear in `list_tools()`. The CI step must enforce this env state explicitly.'

findings.finding: The plan requires the reference to 'distinguish the read-only vs write (live) profiles and note which tools are gated by REBAR_MCP_READONLY / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC,' but provides no decision rule for how the generator discovers which gate governs which tool. The three gates have fundamentally different enforcement semantics: REBAR_MCP_READONLY is a registration-time gate (write tools absent from list_tools()), while REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM and REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC are call-time gates (tools always registered). Furthermore, `sign_review` in `_mcp_llm.py` lines 222-242 is annotated MUTATE and gated on `_readonly()` — not `_allow_llm` — making it a hybrid that the plan's three-category schema cannot correctly classify. No metadata attribute, source-code pattern, or lookup table is specified for the generator to use.
findings.location: Plan body: 'It MUST distinguish the read-only vs write (live) profiles and note which tools are gated by REBAR_MCP_READONLY / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'

findings.finding: The plan asserts the generator will 'distinguish the read-only vs write (live) profiles and note which tools are gated by REBAR_MCP_READONLY / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC' from the live tool registry, but REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM gates at call time (not registration time) — LLM tools always appear in list_tools() regardless of that flag's value. A single build_server().list_tools() invocation cannot signal which tools are LLM-gated; the plan's claimed capability is partially unverified as described.
findings.location: Plan body — 'It MUST distinguish the read-only vs write (live) profiles and note which tools are gated by REBAR_MCP_READONLY / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM / REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC'
findings.suggested_fix: Drive two build_server() invocations: one with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0 + REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM=0 (baseline) and one with REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM=1, then diff the sets — but since LLM tools are always registered, this won't help. Instead, read `tool.annotations` from the FastMCP Tool objects (FastMCP 1.9+ populates annotations from the `annotations=` kwarg), and cross-reference against the `_ANN` preset keys (READ_ONLY_OPEN_WORLD = LLM-capable tools) or maintain a small lookup table in the generator keyed off tool name.

findings.finding: None of the four acceptance criteria include a concrete proving command. An executor cannot demonstrate completion without commands that produce observable evidence (e.g., running the generator, grepping the output, triggering the drift gate).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four bullets)

findings.finding: `CLAUDE.md` contains a hand-maintained 'MCP tool set' section (lines 133–184) that the plan explicitly claims this ticket supersedes ('the hand-maintained catalog in CLAUDE.md cannot drift'), but `CLAUDE.md` is absent from the Files list and not mentioned as an edit target. If the generated reference replaces it, `CLAUDE.md` must be updated to redirect to `docs/mcp-reference.md`; if it is intentionally left as-is, the 'cannot drift' claim is false.
findings.location: Files section / plan body ('the hand-maintained catalog in CLAUDE.md cannot drift')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `CLAUDE.md` to the Files section and add an AC or plan note that the '## MCP tool set' section is replaced by a one-line redirect: e.g. '**MCP tool set:** see [`docs/mcp-reference.md`](docs/mcp-reference.md) (generated).'

findings.finding: AC2 uses the word 'correctly' as the sole quality predicate ('The reference correctly separates read-only from write/live-gated tools') without naming a test oracle or specifying what a correct separation looks like structurally (separate sections? column in a table? badge?). This makes the criterion subjective and un-automatable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 2

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
